attributes.stem: A 60-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of paroxysmal flushing of the face, neck, and upper trunk for the past two months, often triggered by stress and certain foods. The flushing episodes last for 5 to 7 minutes. She also complains of chronic, watery diarrhoea and abdominal cramping. She has a history of smoking which she quit 1 year ago due to shortness of breath. She regularly takes perindopril for hypertension and her blood pressure was recorded as normal during the episodes of flushing. On examination, she has peri-orbital swelling and increased lacrimation during flushing episodes. She has a blood pressure of 110/70 mm Hg and a pulse of 92 per min. Echocardiography done for shortness of breath reveals plaque-like deposits on the tricuspid valve.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The patient's symptoms of recurrent paroxysmal flushing, chronic watery diarrhoea, abdominal cramping, and peri-orbital swelling suggest a possibility of carcinoid syndrome. Additionally, the presence of plaque-like deposits on the tricuspid valve (which is characteristic of carcinoid heart disease) supports this diagnosis.

Carcinoid syndrome arises from neuroendocrine tumours, previously termed carcinoid tumours, predominantly found in the gastrointestinal and respiratory tracts. These tumours release many bioactive substances, including serotonin and histamine, into the bloodstream. Hepatic metastasis commonly allows these substances to evade first-pass metabolism, leading to systemic effects such as flushing, diarrhoea, and cardiac complications. The pathophysiology involves the systemic circulation of these substances, triggering vasodilation, increased gastrointestinal motility, and fluid secretion. Diagnosis relies on assessing biomarkers like 24-hour urine for 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), serum chromogranin A, and imaging studies for tumour localization. Management strategies focus on symptom control and tumour management to improve patient outcomes.

 Allergic reactions typically involve itching, hives, swelling of the face or throat, difficulty breathing, and in severe cases, anaphylaxis. While allergic reactions can sometimes cause flushing, the chronic nature of the patient's symptoms (including chronic watery diarrhoea and abdominal cramping) and the absence of typical allergic symptoms make this option less likely.

 Anxiety disorders can manifest with symptoms such as palpitations, sweating, trembling, and shortness of breath, particularly during episodes of anxiety or stress. While stress can trigger symptoms in various conditions, the combination of paroxysmal flushing, chronic watery diarrhoea, abdominal cramping, and the presence of plaque-like deposits on the tricuspid valve suggest a more physical cause rather than solely psychological.

Irritable bowel syndrome is a functional gastrointestinal disorder characterized by abdominal pain or discomfort and altered bowel habits without any evidence of underlying damage to the gastrointestinal tract. While IBS can cause symptoms such as abdominal cramping and changes in bowel habits, it typically does not cause paroxysmal flushing, lacrimation, or peri-orbital swelling. Additionally, the plaque-like deposits on the tricuspid valve are not associated with IBS or Crohn’s disease.

Phaeochromocytoma is less likely due to several factors. Firstly, phaeochromocytoma typically manifests with hypertension, whereas the patient's blood pressure is within the normal range. Secondly, phaeochromocytoma typically presents with paroxysms accompanied by palpitations, headache, and sweating. Furthermore, there are no signs of catecholamine excess like dilated pupils or tremors. The echocardiographic finding of plaque-like deposits on the tricuspid valve is more indicative of carcinoid syndrome than phaeochromocytoma.
learningPoints: Carcinoid syndrome is a cluster of symptoms resulting from the systemic release of bioactive substances by neuroendocrine tumours, predominantly from the gastrointestinal and respiratory tracts.

The pathophysiology of carcinoid syndrome involves the secretion of substances such as serotonin and histamine into the bloodstream, often due to metastasis to the liver, leading to systemic effects like flushing, diarrhoea, and cardiac complications.
Diagnosis of carcinoid syndrome involves assessing biomarkers such as 24-hour urine for 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), serum chromogranin A levels, and imaging studies for tumour localization.

Treatment of carcinoid syndrome focuses on symptom management, including the use of somatostatin analogues, serotonin antagonists, and surgical interventions to control tumour growth and alleviate symptoms.
references: Pandit S, Annamaraju P, Bhusal K. Carcinoid Syndrome. StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448096/

Rubin de Celis Ferrari AC, Glasberg J, Riechelmann RP. Carcinoid syndrome: update on the pathophysiology and treatment. Clinics (Sao Paulo). 2020;73(suppl 1):e490s. Avilable at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30133565/
Gade AK, Olariu E, Douthit NT. Carcinoid Syndrome: A Review. Cureus. 2020;12(3):e7186. Available at:  https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7124884/

Duarte B, Diana & G.Martins, Raquel. Carcinoid syndrome: a review. Revista Portuguesa de Endocrinologia, Diabetes e Metabolismo. 2022. Available at: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/362761795_Carcinoid_syndrome_a_review
